title: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.title: Prevalence and Transmission Patterns
items.content: Disorders of amino acid metabolism, though individually rare, collectively affect a significant number of newborns. They are primarily transmitted as autosomal recessive traits.
keyPoints: Incidence ranges from 1 in 10,000 for cystinuria or phenylketonuria to 1 in 200,000 for homocystinuria or alkaptonuria.
Collectively affect approximately 1 in 4,000 newborns.
Almost all disorders are transmitted as autosomal recessive traits.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Approaches
items.content: The clinical manifestations of amino acid metabolism disorders vary widely, with some causing severe CNS dysfunction or being lethal if untreated. Diagnosis often involves plasma amino acids analysis, urine organic acids, and acylcarnitine profiles.
keyPoints: CNS dysfunction is present in more than half the disorders.
Protein-induced vomiting, neurologic dysfunction, and hyperammonemia are common in urea cycle disorders.
Metabolic ketoacidosis often accompanies hyperammonemia in organic acidemias.
Diagnosis typically involves ion-exchange chromatography or liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry for plasma amino acids.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Newborn Screening
items.content: Early diagnosis through newborn screening can prevent or mitigate clinical manifestations by initiating appropriate treatments such as dietary restrictions.
keyPoints: Newborn screening programs aim to identify several disorders early.
Positive screening tests require further metabolic testing for confirmation.
Confirmed cases should be referred to a metabolic center for therapy initiation.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Treatment in Phenylketonuria
items.content: Early diagnosis and dietary management of phenylketonuria (PKU) are crucial to prevent severe intellectual disability. Newborn screening is essential as it allows for the initiation of treatment before significant neurological damage occurs.
keyPoints: Phenylketonuria has a frequency of 1:16,500.
It results from reduced activity of phenylalanine hydroxylase, leading to accumulation of phenylalanine.
Untreated PKU leads to severe intellectual disability and other neurological issues.
Newborn screening is standard in North America, Australia, and Europe using blood phenylalanine levels.
Dietary treatment should start before 2 weeks of age to prevent intellectual disability.
Blood phenylalanine levels >360 μmol/L indicate the need for dietary restriction.
Plasma phenylalanine concentrations should be maintained between 120 and 360 μmol/L with therapy.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.title: Management of Phenylketonuria in Adults and Pregnancy
items.content: Adults with phenylketonuria require ongoing dietary management to prevent cognitive deficits. Women with PKU must strictly control phenylalanine levels before and during pregnancy to minimize risks to the offspring.
keyPoints: Compliance with a low-phenylalanine diet is challenging but necessary to avoid executive function or psychiatric symptoms in adults.
Oral tetrahydrobiopterin (5–20 mg/kg per day) can help reduce phenylalanine levels when combined with dietary management.
Pegvaliaise, an injectable drug, can significantly lower phenylalanine levels, allowing a more normal diet.
Women with PKU must maintain strict phenylalanine control before and during pregnancy to prevent congenital defects and microcephaly in offspring.
Strict phenylalanine restriction should be maintained 2 months prior to conception and throughout gestation.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Classic Homocystinuria
items.content: Classic homocystinuria, characterized by reduced cystathionine β-synthase activity, presents with elevated plasma methionine and free homocysteine. Diagnosis involves plasma amino acid analysis showing elevated methionine and total plasma homocysteine levels usually >100 μM.
keyPoints: Classic homocystinuria frequency: 1:450,000
Elevated total plasma homocysteine: usually >100 μM
Presentation: dislocated optic lenses, intellectual disability (50% of cases), marfanoid habitus, osteoporosis
Vascular complications can be life-threatening during the first decade

Treatment includes a protein and methionine-restricted diet, oral pyridoxine (25–500 mg/d) in about half of patients, and supplementation with folate and vitamin B₁₂. Betaine also reduces homocysteine levels.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.title: Clinical Implications of Hyperhomocysteinemia
items.content: Hyperhomocysteinemia, an elevated total plasma homocysteine concentration, is associated with increased risk for vascular diseases and neurological conditions. It can result from genetic defects or deficiencies in pyridoxine, folic acid, or vitamin B₁₂.
keyPoints: Increased risk of coronary, cerebrovascular, peripheral arterial disease, deep-vein thrombosis
Associated with neural tube defects, dementia (Alzheimer's type), and Parkinson's disease
Risk reduction: Vitamin B₁₂, folic acids, pyridoxine supplements can reduce homocysteine levels
Significant risk reduction of stroke when plasma homocysteine >30 μM
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Alkaptonuria
items.content: Alkaptonuria is a rare disorder characterized by the accumulation of homogentisic acid, leading to ochronosis. Diagnosis can be suspected in patients with urine that darkens upon standing or alkali addition. Confirmation involves identifying homogentisic acid via urine organic acid analysis.
keyPoints: Frequency: 1:250,000
Symptoms include darkening of urine and connective tissue pigmentation.
Ochronotic arthritis typically begins between ages 30-40.
Nitisinone (10 mg/d) reduces homogentisic acid excretion and delays progression.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Urea Cycle Defects in Adults
items.content: Urea cycle defects can manifest as hyperammonemia, with symptoms ranging from protein avoidance and mood swings to severe neurological impairment. Females with ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency may present during childbirth.
keyPoints: Affect ~1:35,000 individuals
Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is X-linked and most common.
Symptoms include protein avoidance, vomiting, mood swings, and potential coma.
Diagnosis involves plasma ammonia, amino acids, and urine orotic acid measurement.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Hyperammonemia
items.content: Hyperammonemia can result from urea cycle defects, liver disease, organic acidemias, or fatty acid oxidation defects. Diagnosis requires specific biochemical tests.
keyPoints: Increased plasma glutamine is common in all urea cycle defects.
Citrulline levels help differentiate between proximal and distal defects.
Urine orotic acid is elevated only in ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency.
Plasma acylcarnitine profile helps exclude fatty acid oxidation defects.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.title: Management of Acute Hyperammonemia in Urea Cycle Disorders
items.content: In acute hyperammonemia due to urea cycle defects, immediate management includes stopping catabolism and ammonia production by providing adequate calories via IV glucose and lipids. Insulin may be needed if required. Excess nitrogen is removed using IV phenylacetate and benzoate, which conjugates with glutamine and glycine to form excretable compounds. Arginine supplementation (200 mg/kg per day) is essential for resuming protein synthesis unless in arginase deficiency. If ammonia levels do not decrease, hemodialysis should be initiated promptly.
keyPoints: Provide IV glucose and lipids to stop catabolism and ammonia production.
Use IV phenylacetate (0.25 g/kg priming dose) and benzoate for nitrogen removal.
Administer arginine at 200 mg/kg per day unless in arginase deficiency.
Initiate hemodialysis if ammonia levels remain high.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.title: Chronic Management and Considerations for Urea Cycle Disorders
items.content: For chronic management of urea cycle defects, a protein-restricted diet is essential. Treatment options include phenylbutyrate, glycerol phenylbutyrate (better tolerated), arginine or citrulline supplements, depending on the specific diagnosis. Oral carglumic acid can restore functional urea cycling in N-acetylglutamate synthase deficiency, making other therapies unnecessary. Liver transplantation should be considered for severe cases uncontrolled by medical therapy.
keyPoints: Implement a protein-restricted diet for chronic management.
Use phenylbutyrate or glycerol phenylbutyrate, arginine/citrulline supplements based on diagnosis.
Carglumic acid can restore urea cycle function in N-acetylglutamate synthase deficiency.
Consider liver transplantation for severe, medically uncontrolled cases.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.question: Which of the following is NOT an essential amino acid?
items.explanation: Glycine is not an essential amino acid as it can be synthesized endogenously.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.question: What is the typical mode of inheritance for most disorders of amino acid metabolism?
items.explanation: Almost all inherited disorders of amino acid metabolism are transmitted as autosomal recessive traits.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.question: Which diagnostic method is commonly used to analyze plasma amino acids?
items.explanation: Plasma amino acids are commonly analyzed by ion-exchange chromatography or liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation in many disorders of the urea cycle?
items.explanation: Protein-induced vomiting, neurologic dysfunction, and hyperammonemia occur in many disorders of the urea cycle.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.question: What is the primary enzyme deficiency in phenylketonuria (PKU)?
items.explanation: PKU results from reduced activity of phenylalanine hydroxylase.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.question: What is the recommended blood phenylalanine level to initiate dietary treatment in newborns with PKU?
items.explanation: Dietary phenylalanine restriction is usually instituted if blood phenylalanine levels are >360 μmol/L.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.question: Which drug can be used to reduce phenylalanine levels in PKU patients by converting it to trans-cinnamic acid and ammonia?
items.explanation: Pegvaliaise is a pegylated form of phenylalanine ammonia lyase that converts phenylalanine to trans-cinnamic acid and ammonia.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is most commonly associated with classic homocystinuria?
items.explanation: Classic homocystinuria is most commonly due to reduced activity of cystathionine β-synthase.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.question: Which treatment can lead to a reduction in plasma methionine and homocysteine concentration in some patients with classic homocystinuria?
items.explanation: In approximately half of the patients with classic homocystinuria, oral pyridoxine can reduce plasma methionine and homocysteine levels.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.question: What is the typical presentation age range for classic homocystinuria?
items.explanation: Most patients with classic homocystinuria present between 3 and 5 years of age.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is responsible for alkaptonuria?
items.explanation: Alkaptonuria is caused by a deficiency of homogentisate 1,2-dioxygenase.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.question: What symptom may herald the onset of ochronotic arthritis in alkaptonuria?
options: Pain and stiffness in small joints
Pain, stiffness, and limitation of motion in hips, knees, and shoulders
items.explanation: Ochronotic arthritis is heralded by pain, stiffness, and some limitation of motion of the hips, knees, and shoulders.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.question: Which treatment reduces urinary excretion of homogentisic acid in alkaptonuria?
items.explanation: Nitisinone reduces urinary excretion of homogentisic acid in alkaptonuria.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.question: What is a common feature of urea cycle defects?
options: Direct neuronal toxicity and brain edema
items.explanation: Accumulation of ammonia in urea cycle defects leads to direct neuronal toxicity and brain edema.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.question: Which compound is used to conjugate with glycine in the treatment of urea cycle defects?
items.explanation: Benzoate conjugates with glycine to form hippuric acid, which is excreted in the urine.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.question: What is the role of arginine supplementation in patients with urea cycle defects?
options: It acts as a nitrogen scavenger.
It becomes an essential amino acid and supports protein synthesis.
items.explanation: Arginine becomes an essential amino acid in urea cycle defects (except in arginase deficiency) and is provided to resume protein synthesis.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.question: In which specific condition can oral carglumic acid render other therapies unnecessary?
items.explanation: Oral carglumic acid can restore a functional urea cycle in patients with N-acetylglutamate synthase deficiency.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.statement: Complete deficiency of ornithine transcarbamylase is usually not lethal.
items.explanation: Complete deficiency of ornithine transcarbamylase is lethal in the untreated neonate.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.statement: Newborn screening programs can help prevent or mitigate clinical manifestations if a diagnosis is achieved early.
items.explanation: The clinical manifestations in many conditions can be prevented or mitigated with early diagnosis and appropriate treatment.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.statement: Untreated children with PKU are normal at birth but develop severe intellectual disability if untreated.
items.explanation: Children with classic phenylketonuria appear normal at birth but fail to attain developmental milestones and show progressive cerebral impairment if untreated.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.statement: Pegvaliaise is derived from human enzymes.
items.explanation: Pegvaliaise is a pegylated form of phenylalanine ammonia lyase, which is a bacterial enzyme.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HYPERPHENYLALANINEMIAS | Phenylketonuria

items.statement: Elevated levels of methionine can be detected by neonatal screening for classic homocystinuria.
items.explanation: Neonatal screening can detect elevated levels of methionine, although milder variants may be missed.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.statement: Hyperhomocysteinemia is always associated with significant homocystinuria.
items.explanation: Hyperhomocysteinemia can occur without significant homocystinuria, such as in heterozygotes for genetic defects.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · THE HOMOCYSTINURIAS (HYPERHOMOCYSTEINEMIAS)

items.statement: Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is an autosomal recessive disorder.
items.explanation: Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is X-linked, not autosomal recessive.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.statement: Arginine levels are markedly elevated in argininosuccinic acid synthase deficiency.
items.explanation: Plasma citrulline is markedly increased, not arginine, in argininosuccinic acid synthase deficiency.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.statement: Liver transplantation is considered for all patients with urea cycle defects.
items.explanation: Liver transplantation should be considered in severe cases that are difficult to control medically, not all cases.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.statement: Hyperammonemia can occur due to a functional deficiency of glutamine synthase.
items.explanation: Hyperammonemia can indeed result from a functional deficiency of glutamine synthase, especially in patients receiving chemotherapy or undergoing transplants.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Urea Cycle Defects

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.assertion: Defects in the synthesis of nonessential amino acids predominantly affect the brain.
items.reason: Nonessential amino acid defects involve multiple enzymes and can cause neurologic symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these defects predominantly affect the brain.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.subtopic: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults
items.assertion: All inherited disorders of amino acid metabolism present with severe clinical symptoms.
items.reason: Some disorders like sarcosinemia produce no clinical consequences.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all disorders present with severe symptoms; some, such as sarcosinemia, have no clinical consequences.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults

items.assertion: Ochronotic arthritis can be treated with arthroplasty.
items.reason: Arthroplasty is a surgical procedure to replace joints, which helps in managing joint disease symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS

items.assertion: Increased plasma glutamine is seen in all urea cycle defects.
items.reason: Ammonia not removed by the urea cycle is conjugated to glutamate, forming glutamine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inherited Disorders of Amino Acid Metabolism in Adults · ALKAPTONURIA | UREA CYCLE DEFECTS
